network.overall_strategy: Threonine (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanoic acid, C4H9NO3) is synthesized via a multi-environment prebiotic network. The strategy begins with hydrothermal CO2 reduction to C1 units (formate, formaldehyde, CO) and C3 units (pyruvate) on iron-sulfur minerals. These feed into surface aldol, formose, and Strecker chemistry to build the C4 backbone and install the amino and hydroxyl groups. Two major convergence hubs are lactaldehyde (the Strecker aldehyde precursor) and 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate (the reductive amination precursor). Ten distinct pathways span hydrothermal carbon fixation, Fischer-Tropsch synthesis, formose sugar chemistry, Strecker synthesis, phosphoro-Strecker synthesis, cyanosulfidic chemistry, reductive amination of alpha-keto acids, glyoxylate aldol pathways, decarboxylation-aldol sequences, and wet-dry peptide assembly. Hub molecules (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, HCN, lactaldehyde) interconnect across all three environments.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluids from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO2 adsorbs on greigite surface Fe-Ni-S clusters, analogous to biological [Fe4S4] cofactors. H2 dissociatively adsorbs, and surface hydride transfers to CO2, yielding formate via a surface-bound formate intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is well established as a prebiotic carbon fixation pathway at alkaline hydrothermal vents, analogous to the acetyl-CoA pathway in acetogens. Greigite is structurally similar to biological iron-sulfur clusters (Preiner et al., 2020; Yamaguchi et al., 2014). The Lost City hydrothermal field provides H2-rich, alkaline conditions favorable for this reaction (Sources 15866, 2626).

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on mackinawite
reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced via surface hydride on mackinawite FeS layers. The carboxylate oxygen is activated on the mineral surface, and a two-electron reduction yields formaldehyde and water.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential reduction of CO2 → formate → formaldehyde on iron-sulfur minerals follows the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway analogy. Mackinawite is a precursor to greigite and provides layered FeS surfaces for further reduction steps (Roldan et al., 2015). Formaldehyde is a critical C1 building block for prebiotic chemistry (Sources 15634, 6942).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions, CO/H2 gas phase on magnetite surface

reactions.mechanism: CO adsorbs on magnetite surface, undergoes stepwise hydrogenation via dissociated H2 to form surface methyl and methylene species. Chain growth produces C2 oxygenates (acetaldehyde) alongside C1 oxygenates (formaldehyde).
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite catalyzes FTT reactions producing aldehydes, alcohols, and hydrocarbons from CO + H2. Magnetite forms at ~360K and is catalytically active for FTT synthesis (Sources 19309, 19315). The Cold Bokkeveld chondrite containing magnetite catalyzes FTT reactions. Acetaldehyde is a key C2 building block for threonine synthesis.

reactions.name: CO2 fixation to pyruvate on greigite/magnetite

conditions.catalyst: greigite and magnetite mixed mineral assemblage
conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, multi-step CO2 fixation

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction: CO2 → formate → CO → acetyl intermediate → pyruvate. Greigite mediates the initial reduction steps while magnetite supports C-C coupling. Pyruvate forms via carboxylation of an acetyl-mineral intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2 on iron-sulfur/iron-oxide mineral assemblages has been demonstrated as a prebiotic analog of the acetyl-CoA pathway. Pyruvate is a central metabolic hub connecting carbon fixation to amino acid synthesis (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2017). Sources (995, 9332, 9334) identify pyruvate as a prebiotically relevant hub molecule.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: thermal decarboxylation, mildly acidic hydrothermal fluids

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes thermal decarboxylation on the pyrite surface. The C2-C3 bond cleaves heterolytically, releasing CO2 and producing acetaldehyde. Pyrite surface stabilizes the transition state via Lewis acid interactions with the keto carbonyl.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of alpha-keto acids is a well-known prebiotic reaction, producing aldehydes that serve as Strecker synthesis substrates. This provides a second convergent route to acetaldehyde from pyruvate (Sources 6940, 995). Pyrite surfaces catalyze such decarboxylation reactions at hydrothermal temperatures.

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to glyoxylate on elemental iron

conditions.other: strongly reducing conditions, native iron surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Fe0 provides electrons for CO2 reduction. Two CO2 molecules are sequentially reduced and coupled on the Fe0 surface to yield glyoxylate (OHCCOOH), the simplest alpha-keto acid.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is identified as a prebiotically relevant input alongside pyruvate (Sources 995, 9332, 9334). Fe0 is a strong reductant for CO2 fixation and has been shown to drive reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids (Sources 1026). Glyoxylate formation from CO2 on metallic iron provides the C2 keto-acid needed for aldol routes to threonine.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde dimerization to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline evaporitic pool, formose reaction conditions

reactions.mechanism: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. Ca(OH)2 provides alkalinity; borate minerals stabilize the glycolaldehyde product by forming borate esters, preventing further uncontrolled oligomerization.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is a canonical prebiotic pathway for sugar synthesis from formaldehyde. Borate minerals stabilize glycolaldehyde and other sugars, channeling the reaction toward biologically relevant products (Ricardo et al., 2004; Benner et al., 2012). This is the entry point for the formose-to-lactaldehyde pathway to threonine (Sources 15634).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation: glycolaldehyde + formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde enolizes on the montmorillonite surface; the enolate attacks the formaldehyde carbonyl in a crossed aldol reaction, yielding glyceraldehyde. Borate complexation stabilizes the triose product against retro-aldol.
reactions.reasoning: The aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde with formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde is a key step in the formose reaction network. Montmorillonite catalyzes such condensations (Sources 9431). Glyceraldehyde is a critical C3 intermediate en route to methylglyoxal and then lactaldehyde.

reactions.name: Dehydration of glyceraldehyde to methylglyoxal

conditions.other: UV irradiation, no ozone shield, shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes UV-assisted dehydration on TiO2 surface. The C2-OH is eliminated as water, generating the alpha-dicarbonyl methylglyoxal (pyruvaldehyde). TiO2 surface generates radical intermediates that facilitate the elimination.
reactions.reasoning: Methylglyoxal is a known product of glyceraldehyde dehydration and a formose reaction intermediate. UV photocatalysis on TiO2 drives dehydration reactions under early Earth surface conditions with strong UV radiation and no ozone layer (Sources 21187). Methylglyoxal is a direct precursor to lactaldehyde.

reactions.name: Reduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde on sulfide minerals

conditions.other: mildly reducing surface pool, H2S as reductant

reactions.mechanism: Methylglyoxal (CH3COCHO) is selectively reduced at the more electrophilic keto carbonyl. H2S donates electrons via the sulfide mineral surface, reducing the C2 keto group to a hydroxyl, yielding lactaldehyde (CH3CH(OH)CHO) while preserving the aldehyde function.
reactions.reasoning: Selective reduction of alpha-dicarbonyls is facilitated by sulfide minerals acting as electron mediators. H2S provides reducing equivalents in cyanosulfidic chemistry (Sources 9381, 9382, 10209). The selective reduction of one carbonyl over another on mineral surfaces is analogous to enzymatic selectivity.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH3 to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with NH3 to form an imine (Schiff base) intermediate. HCN then undergoes nucleophilic addition to the imine C=N bond, yielding the alpha-aminonitrile (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile). Montmorillonite concentrates the reactants and stabilizes the imine intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker reaction is the most widely studied prebiotic pathway for amino acid synthesis (Sources 6940, 6942, 985). Lactaldehyde is the appropriate aldehyde for threonine formation. Imine-like intermediates are short-lived under aqueous conditions but are driven to completion (Sources 1026). Montmorillonite clays concentrate and catalyze such reactions (Sources 9431).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis, mildly acidic to neutral pH, wet-dry cycling may concentrate products

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-CN) of the aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to an amide (-CONH2), then to a carboxylic acid (-COOH), releasing ammonia. This yields threonine (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanoic acid).
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the terminal step of the Strecker synthesis and occurs readily in aqueous solution at moderate temperatures and neutral to mildly acidic pH (Wolman et al., 1971; Sources 6940, 23270). This completes the Strecker route to threonine.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glyoxylate and acetaldehyde to 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate
reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde enolizes in the slightly alkaline phosphate-buffered environment. The enolate attacks the aldehyde carbonyl of glyoxylate in a crossed aldol condensation, forming the C4 alpha-keto-beta-hydroxy acid (2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate), the alpha-keto acid corresponding to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate and pyruvate are identified as prebiotically relevant inputs in protometabolic networks (Sources 995, 9332, 9334). Aldol condensation of alpha-keto acids with aldehydes produces higher alpha-keto acids that serve as reductive amination substrates. This mirrors the biological connection between TCA cycle intermediates and amino acid biosynthesis (Sources 6987, 6944).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine on Fe0
reactions.mechanism: NH3 attacks the keto carbonyl of 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate, forming a transient imine (Schiff base). Fe0 provides reducing equivalents to reduce the C=N bond to C-NH2, completing the reductive amination to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids by NH3 on Fe0 under aqueous conditions (pH < 7) is a demonstrated prebiotic pathway for amino acid synthesis (Sources 1026). Fe0 serves as both reductant and surface catalyst. This mimics biological transamination and represents the alpha-keto acid route to threonine (Sources 6940, 6943, 6987).

reactions.name: Phosphoro-Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + DAP + HCN to threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: neutral pH, DAP as phosphorylating/activating agent

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with DAP to form an N-phosphoroamino intermediate, which then reacts with HCN to yield N-phosphoroaminonitrile. Under acidic conditions (pH 1.5), the phosphoro group is lost yielding the free aminonitrile. The pathway selectively targets aldehydes over ketones, avoiding unnatural alpha,alpha-disubstituted amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: The phosphoro-Strecker synthesis is a prebiotically plausible variation of the Strecker reaction that uses diamidophosphate (DAP) as an activating agent (Sources 21192, 21193). This pathway operates at neutral pH and selectively produces aminonitriles from aldehydes, providing a second convergent route to the threonine aminonitrile intermediate.

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis from acetaldehyde to threonine precursor via cyanosulfidic intermediate

conditions.other: UV irradiation, cyanosulfidic conditions, H2S/HCN co-present

reactions.mechanism: In the cyanosulfidic scenario, acetaldehyde reacts with HCN to form cyanohydrin (lactonitrile). Under UV irradiation, H2S-mediated reduction generates the hydroxylated aldehyde (lactaldehyde) via a series of radical and sulfide-mediated transformations. This provides an alternative pathway from acetaldehyde to the key Strecker aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The cyanosulfidic scenario (Patel et al., 2015; Sutherland and co-workers) provides UV-driven pathways from simple feedstocks (HCN, H2S, aldehydes) to amino acid precursors (Sources 9381, 9382, 9376, 16400). This represents an alternative surface route to lactaldehyde, complementing the formose-based pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 → Formate → Formaldehyde → Formose → Strecker Route
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to formate at hydrothermal vents on greigite, then further reduced to formaldehyde on mackinawite. Formaldehyde is transported to surface pools where formose chemistry builds glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde → methylglyoxal → lactaldehyde. Strecker synthesis with HCN and NH3 on lactaldehyde produces the aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch Acetaldehyde → Cyanosulfidic → Strecker Route
pathways.description: CO and H2 undergo FTT synthesis on magnetite to yield acetaldehyde and formaldehyde. Acetaldehyde enters the cyanosulfidic pathway on surface sulfide minerals under UV to produce lactaldehyde. Strecker synthesis then yields the aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Pyruvate → Decarboxylation → Acetaldehyde → Cyanosulfidic → Strecker
pathways.description: CO2 is fixed to pyruvate on greigite/magnetite at hydrothermal vents. Pyruvate is thermally decarboxylated on pyrite to yield acetaldehyde. Acetaldehyde enters the cyanosulfidic pathway to produce lactaldehyde, then Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis yield threonine.

pathways.name: Glyoxylate + Acetaldehyde Aldol → Reductive Amination Route
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to glyoxylate on Fe0 at hydrothermal vents. Acetaldehyde (from FTT or pyruvate decarboxylation) undergoes aldol condensation with glyoxylate on montmorillonite surfaces to form 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate. Reductive amination on Fe0 yields threonine directly.

pathways.name: Pyruvate → Acetaldehyde → Glyoxylate Aldol → Reductive Amination
pathways.description: Pyruvate formed by hydrothermal CO2 fixation is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde. Separately, glyoxylate is produced on Fe0. The aldol condensation of acetaldehyde with glyoxylate on montmorillonite yields 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate, which is reductively aminated to threonine on Fe0.

pathways.name: Phosphoro-Strecker Route via Formose Lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Formaldehyde from hydrothermal reduction feeds into the formose reaction to produce glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde → methylglyoxal → lactaldehyde. Lactaldehyde reacts with DAP and HCN via the phosphoro-Strecker synthesis to form the aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Phosphoro-Strecker Route via Cyanosulfidic Lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde from FTT synthesis enters the cyanosulfidic pathway to yield lactaldehyde. Lactaldehyde then reacts via the phosphoro-Strecker synthesis with DAP and HCN, followed by hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Pure Hydrothermal: CO2 → Glyoxylate + FTT Acetaldehyde → Keto Acid → Threonine
pathways.description: Both glyoxylate (from Fe0-mediated CO2 reduction) and acetaldehyde (from FTT on magnetite) are produced in the hydrothermal environment. Their aldol condensation at surface interfaces and subsequent reductive amination at hydrothermal Fe0 surfaces complete the synthesis entirely through hydrothermal/near-vent chemistry.

pathways.name: Formose Shortcut: Formaldehyde → Glycolaldehyde → Methylglyoxal → Lactaldehyde → Strecker
pathways.description: Formaldehyde from either hydrothermal or FTT sources enters the formose reaction at the surface, producing glycolaldehyde. Glycolaldehyde condenses with formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde, which dehydrates to methylglyoxal and is selectively reduced to lactaldehyde. The Strecker synthesis completes the path to threonine.

pathways.name: Dual Convergence: Pyruvate Decarboxylation + Formose Merge at Lactaldehyde → Strecker
pathways.description: This pathway merges two convergent routes to lactaldehyde: (1) the formose chain from hydrothermal formaldehyde through glyceraldehyde and methylglyoxal, and (2) the pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde feeding into the cyanosulfidic route. Both converge at lactaldehyde, which undergoes Strecker synthesis to form threonine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-structured pathway with clean Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical flow. The weakest link is rxn_010 (selective reduction of methylglyoxal ketone by H2S on sulfide minerals to give lactaldehyde), which lacks strong mechanistic precedent for selective carbonyl reduction. All other steps are well-supported by formose and Strecker chemistry literature.
suggestions: Consider alternative reductants for methylglyoxal→lactaldehyde, e.g., borohydride minerals or photoreduction
Could replace rxn_010 with direct isomerization/reduction pathway with better precedent

pathway_evaluations.feedback: rxn_016 is chemically problematic: acetaldehyde (C2) + HCN (C1) + H2S should yield a cyanohydrin/aminonitrile, not lactaldehyde (C3 aldehyde). The nitrile-to-aldehyde reduction is not mechanistically justified here. Also, CO (mol_006) used in rxn_003 is not produced by any upstream reaction in the network, creating a dangling input.
suggestions: Rewrite rxn_016 to produce lactonitrile (cyanohydrin) which would then need a separate reduction step to lactaldehyde
Add a reaction producing CO from CO2 or formate
Alternatively, route acetaldehyde through a proper aldol with formaldehyde to get lactaldehyde

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde is well-established. However, the pathway inherits the rxn_016 problem. Direct CO2-to-pyruvate (rxn_004) is ambitious but has some experimental support on iron-sulfur minerals. The overall route is weakened by the cyanosulfidic step.
suggestions: Replace rxn_016 with aldol condensation of acetaldehyde + formaldehyde to give lactaldehyde directly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong convergent pathway. Glyoxylate + acetaldehyde aldol to 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate is chemically sound and well-precedented. Reductive amination on Fe0 is supported by literature (keto acid + NH3 on metallic iron). The missing CO source for rxn_003 is a minor topology issue. The pathway mirrors biological biosynthesis logic.
suggestions: Add explicit CO production reaction (e.g., CO2 reduction or water-gas shift)
Specify mineral catalyst for rxn_013 surface aldol

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent pathway that avoids the problematic rxn_016. Uses pyruvate decarboxylation as acetaldehyde source instead of FTT, eliminating the CO orphan issue. Convergent at rxn_013 with glyoxylate. Final reductive amination is well-supported. Good Hydrothermal→Surface→Hydrothermal flow.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalyst for surface aldol rxn_013

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses the phosphoro-Strecker synthesis which is directly supported by literature (DAP + aldehyde + HCN). Clean environmental progression from hydrothermal CO2 fixation through surface formose and phosphoro-Strecker to biochemical hydrolysis. The rxn_010 reduction step remains the weakest link but is somewhat compensated by the strong final steps. Literature explicitly supports this type of pathway.
suggestions: Provide more mechanistic detail for rxn_010
Consider borate-stabilized intermediates in formose steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: While the phosphoro-Strecker terminal steps are strong, the pathway is fatally weakened by rxn_016 producing lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde + HCN + H2S, which is mechanistically unsound.
suggestions: Replace lactaldehyde source with aldol route from acetaldehyde + formaldehyde

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Compact purely hydrothermal route. Glyoxylate + acetaldehyde aldol followed by reductive amination is chemically robust. The CO source issue for rxn_003 is again a gap. Environmental progression is limited since it stays mostly hydrothermal, which is both a strength (simplicity) and weakness (less environmental diversity).
suggestions: Clarify whether rxn_013 aldol truly occurs under hydrothermal or surface conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Clean formose-Strecker route starting from formaldehyde. Assumes formaldehyde is available, skipping hydrothermal steps. rxn_010 remains the weakest link. Otherwise chemically sound with well-established formose and Strecker reactions.

Most comprehensive pathway demonstrating network convergence. Dual hydrothermal inputs (formate→formaldehyde and pyruvate→acetaldehyde) merge at lactaldehyde for Strecker synthesis. Shows good network interconnectivity. Still limited by rxn_010 quality, but the overall strategy is sound and well-aligned with multi-environment prebiotic models.
suggestions: Consider adding alternative convergence at lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde + formaldehyde aldol

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed synthesis network with strong topology, good convergence, and solid literature alignment. The network correctly identifies and implements the major prebiotic routes to threonine: Strecker synthesis, phosphoro-Strecker synthesis, and reductive amination of α-keto acids, all of which are explicitly supported by the literature. Hub molecules (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, HCN, lactaldehyde) are well-chosen and properly interconnected. The environmental Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical flow is well-implemented in most pathways. Two significant weaknesses: (1) rxn_016 (cyanosulfidic route to lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde + HCN + H2S) is mechanistically unsound—the product should be a cyanohydrin, not an aldehyde, which undermines pathways P2, P3, and P7; (2) CO (mol_006) is used as input in rxn_003 but never produced by any network reaction, creating a dangling dependency. Additionally, rxn_010 (H2S reduction of methylglyoxal) lacks strong precedent for selective ketone reduction. Despite these issues, 6 of 10 pathways achieve approval, and the strongest routes (P5, P6, P4) are chemically robust and well-supported by literature. The inclusion of DAP-mediated phosphoro-Strecker chemistry is a notable strength, directly supported by recent literature.
